meta.certificationName: AWS Certified Machine Learning Engineer – Associate
meta.title: AWS-MLE-3: Data Preparation, Orchestration, and Monitoring

domains.name: Deployment and Orchestration of ML Workflows

questions.stem: A machine learning team needs to explore, clean, and transform tabular training data with a visual workflow before training in Amazon SageMaker. Which SageMaker capability BEST fits this requirement?

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler** is correct because it is designed for data preparation and feature engineering workflows before training. **Model Monitor** is for post-deployment monitoring, **Inference Recommender** benchmarks inference options, and **Ground Truth** focuses on data labeling.

questions.reference: [Prepare ML data with Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/data-wrangler.html)

questions.stem: A machine learning team wants a no-code way to profile missing values, apply transformations, and export a repeatable data-preparation flow before model training. Which SageMaker capability should they use?

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler** is correct because it is designed for data preparation and feature engineering workflows before training. **Model Monitor** is for post-deployment monitoring, **Inference Recommender** benchmarks inference options, and **Ground Truth** focuses on data labeling.

questions.reference: [Prepare ML data with Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/data-wrangler.html)

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler** is correct because it is designed for data preparation and feature engineering workflows before training. **Model Monitor** is for post-deployment monitoring, **Inference Recommender** benchmarks inference options, and **Ground Truth** focuses on data labeling.

questions.reference: [Prepare ML data with Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/data-wrangler.html)

questions.reference: [Prepare ML data with Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/data-wrangler.html)

questions.stem: A company wants to compute features once, reuse them across multiple models, and serve the same feature definitions for both training and online inference. Which service should the team use?

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Feature Store** is correct because it centralizes feature definitions and supports both offline and online feature access. **S3 Glacier**, **Athena prepared statements**, and **CloudWatch Logs Insights** do not provide managed ML feature storage for training and serving reuse.

questions.reference: [Create, store, and share features with Amazon SageMaker Feature Store](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/feature-store.html)

questions.stem: A recommendation team wants one managed store for reusable features so the same engineered values can be used during both training and low-latency inference. Which AWS service should they choose?

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Feature Store** is correct because it centralizes feature definitions and supports both offline and online feature access. **S3 Glacier**, **Athena prepared statements**, and **CloudWatch Logs Insights** do not provide managed ML feature storage for training and serving reuse.

questions.reference: [Create, store, and share features with Amazon SageMaker Feature Store](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/feature-store.html)

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Feature Store** is correct because it centralizes feature definitions and supports both offline and online feature access. **S3 Glacier**, **Athena prepared statements**, and **CloudWatch Logs Insights** do not provide managed ML feature storage for training and serving reuse.

questions.reference: [Create, store, and share features with Amazon SageMaker Feature Store](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/feature-store.html)

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Feature Store** is correct because it centralizes feature definitions and supports both offline and online feature access. **S3 Glacier**, **Athena prepared statements**, and **CloudWatch Logs Insights** do not provide managed ML feature storage for training and serving reuse.

questions.stem: A team needs versioned records of trained models, approval states, lineage, and a controlled handoff from experimentation to production. Which SageMaker feature addresses this need MOST directly?

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Model Registry** is correct because it manages model versions, approval status, lineage, and deployment governance. **Neo** optimizes models for target hardware, **EventBridge Scheduler** schedules events, and **EMR Studio** is unrelated to model lifecycle governance.

questions.reference: [Model registration and deployment with Amazon SageMaker Model Registry](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-registry.html)

questions.stem: A governance team wants a managed catalog of model versions, approval status, lineage, and deployment readiness for trained artifacts. Which SageMaker capability should they adopt?

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Model Registry** is correct because it manages model versions, approval status, lineage, and deployment governance. **Neo** optimizes models for target hardware, **EventBridge Scheduler** schedules events, and **EMR Studio** is unrelated to model lifecycle governance.

questions.reference: [Model registration and deployment with Amazon SageMaker Model Registry](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-registry.html)

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Model Registry** is correct because it manages model versions, approval status, lineage, and deployment governance. **Neo** optimizes models for target hardware, **EventBridge Scheduler** schedules events, and **EMR Studio** is unrelated to model lifecycle governance.

questions.reference: [Model registration and deployment with Amazon SageMaker Model Registry](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-registry.html)

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Model Registry** is correct because it manages model versions, approval status, lineage, and deployment governance. **Neo** optimizes models for target hardware, **EventBridge Scheduler** schedules events, and **EMR Studio** is unrelated to model lifecycle governance.

questions.reference: [Model registration and deployment with Amazon SageMaker Model Registry](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-registry.html)

questions.stem: A platform team wants a repeatable ML workflow that chains preprocessing, training, evaluation, approval, and deployment steps with lineage and auditability. Which AWS service is purpose-built for this?

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Pipelines** is correct because it is purpose-built for orchestrating end-to-end ML workflows with SageMaker integrations and lineage. **Step Functions** can orchestrate general workflows but is not the purpose-built ML pipeline service described here. **Kinesis** and **AppConfig** do not provide ML pipeline orchestration.

options.text: AWS Step Functions Express Workflows only

questions.reference: [Amazon SageMaker Pipelines](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/pipelines.html)

questions.stem: A model must handle unpredictable request spikes with minimal infrastructure management and no need to keep idle instances running. Which deployment pattern is MOST appropriate?

options.text: A large multi-AZ self-managed EC2 Auto Scaling group

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Serverless Inference** is correct because it fits intermittent or spiky workloads without managing always-on instances. **Batch transform** is offline inference, and the EC2 or notebook options add unnecessary management overhead for this requirement.

options.text: Notebook instances exposed through a load balancer

questions.reference: [Deploy models for inference - Amazon SageMaker AIDeploy models for inference - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/deploy-model.html)

questions.stem: A retailer needs to score 50 million records overnight and write predictions to Amazon S3. Latency is not important, but operational efficiency is. Which inference option should the team choose?

options.text: A Studio notebook with manual loops
Amazon API Gateway with Lambda only

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Batch Transform** is correct because it is designed for offline, large-scale inference jobs that read input data and write predictions in bulk. A real-time endpoint is optimized for low-latency requests, while notebooks and Lambda-only approaches are poor fits for managed large-scale batch scoring.

options.text: A real-time endpoint with synchronous requests

questions.reference: [Use Batch Transform for offline inference](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/batch-transform.html)

questions.stem: A production team wants automated checks for data quality drift and model quality issues after a model is deployed. Which SageMaker feature should they enable?

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor** is correct because it continuously monitors deployed models for data quality, bias, feature attribution, and model quality signals. **JumpStart** provides solution starters, **DataBrew** prepares data, and **QuickSight Q** is unrelated to ML endpoint monitoring.

questions.reference: [Monitor models in production with Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-monitor.html)

questions.stem: A production ML service needs automatic detection of data drift and model-quality regressions after deployment. Which SageMaker capability should be enabled?

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor** is correct because it continuously monitors deployed models for data quality, bias, feature attribution, and model quality signals. **JumpStart** provides solution starters, **DataBrew** prepares data, and **QuickSight Q** is unrelated to ML endpoint monitoring.

questions.reference: [Monitor models in production with Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-monitor.html)

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor** is correct because it continuously monitors deployed models for data quality, bias, feature attribution, and model quality signals. **JumpStart** provides solution starters, **DataBrew** prepares data, and **QuickSight Q** is unrelated to ML endpoint monitoring.

questions.reference: [Monitor models in production with Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-monitor.html)

questions.stem: A bank must assess whether model predictions differ unfairly across sensitive groups during training and ongoing monitoring. Which SageMaker capability is designed for that analysis?

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Clarify** is correct because it helps detect bias and explain model predictions during pre-training and post-deployment workflows. **Ground Truth Plus** supports labeling, **CloudTrail Lake** stores audit events, and **GuardDuty** is for threat detection.

questions.reference: [Bias drift for models in production - Amazon SageMaker AIBias drift for models in production - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/clarify-model-monitor-bias-drift.html)

questions.stem: A data scientist needs a managed way to run distributed training jobs, store model artifacts in Amazon S3, and track the resulting output for later deployment. Which SageMaker capability should be used?

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker training jobs** are correct because they provide managed training infrastructure and produce model artifacts that can be deployed later. **CloudFront**, **Storage Gateway**, and **Route 53** do not perform model training.

questions.reference: [Model training - Amazon SageMaker AIModel training - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/train-model.html)

questions.stem: An ML platform owner wants users to have only the permissions required to build, train, and deploy approved models. Which security principle should guide the IAM design?

options.text: Rely on network ACLs instead of IAM policies
Use one shared root account for all data scientists

options.explanation: **Apply least-privilege IAM permissions** is correct because AWS recommends granting only the permissions required for each role and workflow. Granting full admin access or relying solely on account-wide or network controls weakens security and governance.

questions.reference: [Amazon SageMaker AI identity-based policy examples - Amazon SageMaker AIAmazon SageMaker AI identity-based policy examples - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/security_iam_id-based-policy-examples.html)
